methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Anyon Systems is a Series A quantum computing company that has raised C$23M+ to date. The valuation universe comprises 11 peers across quantum computing: three public companies (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and eight private firms including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti, D-Wave, and Infleqtion.

Peer-median valuation analysis was not applicable. The quantum computing sector contains only 7 same-sector peers overall, and filtering for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar valuation-tier comparables yielded insufficient datapoints—fewer than the three required for statistical reliability. A sector-wide median would introduce substantial comparability noise given the heterogeneity in business models, technical approaches, and development stage across quantum platforms.

The lack of sufficient narrow-band comparables reflects the fragmentation and early maturity of the quantum computing sector. Without three or more credible same-stage peers, traditional multiples-based triangulation cannot be reliably applied to Anyon Systems. This analysis underscores a key uncertainty in valuing frontier-stage quantum companies: the absence of established market pricing mechanisms or standardized performance metrics. Further valuation work would benefit from alternative methods such as discounted cash flow modeling (conditional on technology milestones) or venture capital method analysis, both of which remain outside the scope of available data here.
